event: Micronesian and Polynesian seafarers settle Nauru, establishing one of the most isolated inhabited islands in the Pacific.
British sea captain John Fearn of the whaling ship Hunter sights the island and names it Pleasant Island.
European and escaped convict beachcombers begin settling on Nauru, introducing firearms and alcohol to the local population.
The Nauruan Tribal War breaks out among the island's twelve clans, fuelled by imported guns and lasting roughly a decade.
Germany annexes Nauru, incorporating it into its Marshall Islands protectorate and disarming the warring clans.
Christian missionaries arrive and begin converting the Nauruan population, while German administrators impose colonial order.
Prospector Albert Ellis identifies the island's rock as high-grade phosphate, revealing Nauru's vast guano deposits.
The Pacific Phosphate Company begins commercial phosphate mining, the start of an industry that would dominate Nauru's economy.
Australian forces occupy Nauru at the outbreak of World War I, ending German colonial rule.
The League of Nations places Nauru under a joint British, Australian and New Zealand mandate administered chiefly by Australia.
The British Phosphate Commissioners take over phosphate operations, exporting the resource to Australia and New Zealand.
German auxiliary cruisers shell Nauru and sink several phosphate ships during World War II, disrupting mining.
Japanese forces occupy Nauru, building an airfield and subjecting Nauruans to forced labour and harsh conditions.
Japan deports about 1,200 Nauruans to Chuuk (Truk) as labourers, where many die before war's end.
Australian forces reoccupy Nauru after Japan's surrender, and surviving deportees are later returned to the island.
Nauru becomes a United Nations Trust Territory administered by Australia on behalf of the UK and New Zealand.
The Nauru Local Government Council is established, giving Nauruans a representative body under Australian administration.
Australia proposes resettling all Nauruans on Curtis Island off Queensland, an offer the Nauruans reject in favour of independence.
A Legislative Council is created, and Hammer DeRoburt emerges as the leading advocate for Nauruan self-determination.
Nauruans gain control of the phosphate industry by agreeing to purchase the British Phosphate Commissioners' assets.
Nauru gains independence on 31 January with Hammer DeRoburt as founding president, becoming the world's smallest republic.
Nauru takes full ownership of phosphate operations through the newly formed Nauru Phosphate Corporation.
Nauru files a case against Australia at the International Court of Justice seeking compensation for environmental damage from phosphate mining.
Australia agrees to a settlement paying Nauru millions in compensation for the rehabilitation of mined-out lands.
Nauru is admitted as a full member of the United Nations on 14 September.
Nauru begins hosting Australia's offshore asylum-seeker detention centre under the Pacific Solution, a major source of revenue.
With phosphate reserves nearly exhausted and finances in crisis, the government declares a state of emergency amid economic collapse.
Air Nauru's sole aircraft is repossessed over debts, isolating the island until a replacement is arranged.
Australia closes the Nauru detention centre, cutting off a key income stream for the island.
Australia reopens the offshore processing centre on Nauru, restoring it as a central pillar of the national economy.
Nauru hosts the Pacific Islands Forum, drawing attention to climate change threats facing low-lying island states.
Nauru's sponsored deep-sea mining company triggers the International Seabed Authority's two-year rule, accelerating talks on ocean-floor mining.
Australia and Nauru sign a new agreement to keep offshore asylum processing capacity operational on the island.
Nauru switches diplomatic recognition from Taiwan to the People's Republic of China days after Taiwan's presidential election.
Nauru and Australia sign a treaty providing budget support and security cooperation in exchange for Australian veto over third-country agreements.
Nauru launches a 'golden passport' citizenship-by-investment scheme to fund relocation of residents threatened by rising seas.
